title: Require borders to serve a structural or interactive purpose
summary: Models add borders as decorative afterthoughts — 1px light-gray dividers between every element, around every card, on every edge — that create visual clutter without communicating grouping, state, or hierarchy.
instruction: Every visible border must serve at least one of: separating distinct content groups, indicating interactive state (hover, focus, selected, error), defining a clickable hit area, or creating deliberate visual emphasis. Do not apply borders to every surface by default. Use spacing or background color as an alternative separator first.
rationale: Borders are a visual cost: they add ink (or pixels), create contrast, and occupy attention. When every element gets a border, the separator signal is drowned in noise. The best border is often no border — let spacing and background gradients do the grouping work.
violations: A card grid where every card has a 1px #E5E7EB border, every card section inside has a 1px #F3F4F6 border, and every divider between them is also a 1px line — triple border accumulation.
A sidebar where every list item has a bottom border, producing a ruled-line effect that makes the navigation feel like a printed form rather than interactive UI.
A dashboard where metric cards, chart containers, table rows, header cells, sidebar items, and footer all have distinct border definitions using different colors and widths.
controls: A card grid using box-shadow for elevation instead of borders, reserving borders exclusively for interactive states (selected card gets a 2px brand-color border).
A dashboard using background alternation (white rows, light-gray rows) to distinguish table rows, with borders only on the table header and footer.
A sidebar using 8px spacing between items and a 1px right border on the sidebar container itself, with no item-level borders.
borderlineCases: A data table where every cell has a 1px border — acceptable in tabular data contexts where the grid structure aids scanning, but the distinction from decorative borders must be documented.
contextualExceptions: Data tables, calendars, schedule grids, and other tabular layouts where cell borders are integral to data readability.
Print stylesheets where borders replace interactive cues (no hover states available on paper).
Form validation where borders indicate field state (error, success, warning) — these are functional, not decorative.
detectorSignal: More than 50% of visible containers in a viewport have a border declaration, or the same border-style appears on 5+ distinct element types
matcherProposal: Count all elements with a non-zero border-width, grouped by element type. Flag if the ratio of bordered elements to total visible elements exceeds 0.5 AND no more than 30% are data-table cells. Compare the page's border usage against its spacing (padding, margin) — high border + low spacing = decorative borders.
detectorLimitations: Cannot determine intent — a border that appears decorative to the detector may be functional for interactive states not visible at static capture (e.g., a default-state border that turns brand color on hover).
Box-shadow cannot always be distinguished from borders without checking the actual property.
CSS reset/normalize stylesheets often apply default borders that components override — detector must check computed style, not authored CSS.
falsePositiveScenarios: A complex data dashboard where many elements need borders for information hierarchy (a Bloomberg terminal style).
A design system with a deliberate border-per-item approach (e.g., a directory listing with ruled lines) that is consistent and documented.
mitigationStrategy: Exclude table cells, form inputs, and data-grid elements. Only flag when at least 5 bordered elements share the same border-style and no grouping or state-dependent purpose is evident. Set to warning severity.

fixturePlans.violation: A marketing page where every card, image, button, input, and text area has a 1px #D1D5DB border — borders on every element, no spacing alternatives used.
fixturePlans.control: A marketing page using 16px spacing between cards, subtle box-shadows for elevation, and borders only on form inputs (for hit-area definition) and the primary CTA (for emphasis).
fixturePlans.borderline: A data dashboard with bordered table cells (functional) and bordered metric cards (decorative) — the detector may flag the metric cards even though the table borders are justified.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
overlapNotes: Covers the borders category. Distinct from radius.everything-pill (shape) and depth.shadow-soup (depth). Borders are a separate visual channel. No overlap.
